title: The Discriminator as a Normal CNN

exercises.prompt: Recall from our previous lessons: What is the main job of the Generator in a GAN?

options.text: It downsamples high-resolution images into random noise.
It takes random noise and scales it up using transposed convolutions to create fake images.
It immediately outputs a final prediction of real or fake without processing.

exercises.explain: We know the Generator uses transposed convolutions to scale up noise into detailed images. Today, we look at the other half: the Discriminator.

cards.body: Imagine you are an art detective inspecting a painting. Instead of trying to guess whether the painting is of a dog, a cat, or a bowl of fruit, you only have one question to answer: Is this a genuine masterpiece, or is it a clever forgery? That's exactly how the Discriminator in a GAN operates!
cards.title: The Art Detective

cards.body: In a Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN), the Discriminator is a standard Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). Instead of classifying images into multiple classes like 'dog', 'cat', or 'car', it is configured to output a single probability score between 0 and 1, representing how likely it is that the input image is real rather than fake.
cards.title: Meet the Discriminator

cards.body: Think of a standard CNN classifier as a judge at a pet show choosing between 'Golden Retriever', 'Poodle', or 'Pug'. The Discriminator, however, is like a security guard at the gate checking tickets: they only look at the ticket and say either 'Valid' (1) or 'Invalid' (0).
cards.title: The Gate Security Guard
Checking a Photo
cards.body: If we pass a real photograph of a cat into the Discriminator, the network runs it through normal convolutional layers to extract features, finally outputting a score close to 0.99 (highly likely to be real). If we feed it a blurry, generated image, it outputs a score close to 0.02 (highly likely to be fake).

cards.title: No Magical Layers Needed
cards.body: Many beginners think the Discriminator needs a completely new, exotic neural network architecture. In reality, it uses the exact same convolutional layers, pooling, and activation functions as a standard image classifier you would use to recognize handwritten digits or animals.
Let's compare how a standard CNN classifier and a Discriminator process an image at their final output layers.
cards.title: Multi-Class vs. Real/Fake

right.text: Outputs a single probability score for authenticity (e.g., Real: 95%, Fake: 5%).

left.text: Outputs probabilities for multiple classes (e.g., Cat: 80%, Dog: 15%, Bird: 5%).

exercises.explain: A standard CNN classifier outputs probabilities for many categories, while the Discriminator outputs a single probability score indicating whether the image is real or fake.

options.text: The Discriminator uses entirely different mathematical operations than convolutions.
Instead of classifying objects into categories, it outputs a single probability of being real.
The Discriminator outputs multiple bounding boxes around fake regions.

exercises.prompt: How does the Discriminator's final output differ from a standard Image Classification CNN?

exercises.explain: True! The Discriminator is built using standard convolutional layers just like standard image classifiers.
exercises.prompt: True or False: Under the hood, the Discriminator uses standard convolutional layers to extract features from the input image.

exercises.explain: The Discriminator takes an image as input and outputs a single probability score between 0 and 1, where 1 means definitely real and 0 means definitely fake.
exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence describing the output of the Discriminator.
exercises.sentence: The Discriminator is a CNN that processes an input image and outputs a single [blank] representing how likely the image is real.

exercises.explain: Scenario A represents a standard classification task (labeling objects). Scenario B is the binary real/fake classification task of a GAN Discriminator.
exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes the action of a Discriminator rather than a standard classifier?

options.text: Scenario A: A model processes an image and labels it 'Bicycle' out of 10 possible choices.
Scenario B: A model analyzes an image and outputs a score of 0.87, indicating it is likely authentic data.
We train it like a standard binary classifier using backpropagation.
We must manually tweak its weights after every image because it cannot learn on its own.
It does not need training; it is perfect from the beginning.

exercises.explain: Because the Discriminator is just a normal CNN classifier, standard training techniques like backpropagation and gradient descent are used to update its weights so it gets better at spotting fakes. In the next lesson, we will see how these two networks train together!
exercises.prompt: Since the Discriminator is essentially a standard CNN binary classifier, how do we train it to distinguish real images from fake ones?

concept: DCGAN's discriminator is a standard CNN classifier that outputs a single probability indicating real vs. fake instead of object classes.
